rationale: Document set exceeds 3000 words across 8 files; central finding ("strong AI signals don't separate humans from AI") is load-bearing for the skill's identity and downstream pattern demotions. All five techniques applied.

findings.title: Causal claim inverted: "strong signals don't separate humans from AI" is not what the data shows
findings.evidence_quote: research L1080: 'The grader's existing strong-signal patterns do not distinguish humans from AI in this genre.'  Same file L1083-L1086: Humans 1.8 mean strong / AI fresh 2.0 / AI rewrite 2.6.  README L78: 'fired on humans and AI roughly equally.'
findings.the_assumption: The headline reframe assumes the data shows strong signals fail to separate humans from AI, justifying demotion of em-dashes / manufactured insight / triads.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The reported numbers do separate the groups: AI rewrite 2.6 > AI fresh 2.0 > Human 1.8 is monotonic and ordered the *correct* way (more AI = more strong flags). The gap between human (1.8) and AI rewrite (2.6) is +44%, which the README itself elsewhere calls 'the load-bearing claim.' So the same data is being read as 'works' (in the Performance block) and 'doesn't work' (in the reframe). Both can't be true. The likely framing problem: comparing human (1.8) to *fresh* AI (2.0) shows a small gap and gets called 'roughly equal'; comparing human (1.8) to *rewrite* AI (2.6) shows a large gap and gets called 'load-bearing.' The collapse to 'strong signals don't work' is selective.
findings.consequence_if_wrong: Demoting em dashes, manufactured insight, and triad density (research L1154-L1160) on the basis that they 'don't separate' would remove signals that actually do separate at the population level. The skill loses real signal in pursuit of a finding that the data doesn't actually support.
findings.falsifying_experiment: Run a per-essay paired test: for each of the 5 topics, does the AI-rewrite essay score higher on strong flags than its human counterpart? If 4/5 or 5/5 do, the signal *does* discriminate and the demotion plan is wrong. The grouped means hide the within-pair pattern.
findings.recommendation: In dev/research/2026-04-29-genre-paired-corpus-findings.md, rewrite the 'Headline finding' to match what the data shows: a gradient (1.8 / 2.0 / 2.6) where AI rewrites do trigger more strong signals than humans. Drop the framing 'do not distinguish.' Replace with 'humans also use these patterns at meaningful rates; treat the signal as density-dependent.' Then re-examine README L78 and the demotion list (research L1154) against the actual per-pair pattern.

findings.title: N=5 per group is too small to support the demotion decisions and "+44%" claim
findings.evidence_quote: research L1083 'Humans (n=5) ... 1.8 ... AI rewrite 2.6'.  README L120 'Human-vs-ai_rewrite flag gap: total -12% / strong +44%'.  research L1163 'n=5 per group. Findings are directional; need larger N before being baked into the grader.'
findings.the_assumption: Per-group means from N=5 are stable enough to (a) headline as '+44%' and (b) drive demotion of multiple grader checks.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: With N=5 and integer-valued counts, a single outlier essay shifts the mean by 0.2. The 1.8 vs 2.6 gap is 4 flag-events spread across 10 essays. A rough binomial-ish back-of-envelope: standard error on a mean of 5 small integer counts is on the order of the standard deviation of the distribution itself. Without reporting per-essay values, stdev across essays, or any confidence bound, neither '+44%' nor 'doesn't separate' is supportable. The README puts '+44%' front-and-centre as a load-bearing number while the underlying research doc admits 'directional; need larger N.' Those two statements contradict each other.
findings.consequence_if_wrong: Pattern demotions (em dashes, manufactured insight, triads, markdown headings, vocabulary diversity — research L1154) are reversal-expensive: once removed from strong / context tiers and rewritten into 'review priority' framing, restoring them requires another rewrite of SKILL.md, the grader voice, and the references. Acting on N=5 means the next iteration with N=10 may reverse the call.
findings.falsifying_experiment: Bootstrap the existing data: resample 5 essays from each group with replacement 1000 times; report 95% CI on the strong-flag means. If the human / AI-rewrite CIs overlap, the +44% headline is unsupported. Defer demotion decisions until N>=10/group with non-overlapping CIs on each demoted pattern individually.
findings.recommendation: Move the +44% number out of README's Performance block (README L120-L127) until CIs are reported, OR caveat it inline as 'directional, N=5/group.' In dev/plans/2026-04-28-human-eyes-implementation.md (or a new decision log) add a gate: 'no pattern demotions land until N>=10 per group AND a per-pattern test shows the human/AI separation is not statistically distinguishable.' Today's implementation plan does not encode this gate.

findings.title: Severe length confound: AI samples are 30-90% shorter than human originals; flag-count comparisons are not length-normalised
findings.evidence_quote: research L1118 'Word count | 3,568 | 1,849 | 1,126'.  research L1164 'AI fresh-writes in this corpus are short relative to the human originals (1,849 mean vs 3,568). Some of the variance differences may be partly length-driven.'  prompts L637 (Tucker Max prompt) requests '~9,700 words'; the AI fresh output is 74 lines (verified) vs the human's 806 lines. The prompt was not honoured.
findings.the_assumption: Mean total / strong flag counts can be compared across groups whose word counts differ by 2-3x, and the comparison reflects pattern density rather than text length.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: Most pattern checks count occurrences with a length-implicit threshold (e.g., em-dash count, markdown heading count, anaphora count). On 3,568 words a human accumulates more chances to fire a check than AI on 1,126 words. If the human's flag rate per 1000 words is the comparison, the picture inverts: human 1.8 strong flags / 3,568 words = 0.50 per 1k vs AI rewrite 2.6 / 1,126 = 2.31 per 1k. That's a ~4.6x gap, not a 1.4x gap. The same length confound makes 'humans use markdown headings 5/5, AI 1/5' (research L1140) look like an inverted signal when in fact AI essays are too short to need section breaks at all.
findings.consequence_if_wrong: The 'inverted signals' (markdown headings, vocabulary diversity, manufactured insight) may not be inverted at all once length is controlled. The reframe ('these patterns are register-coded') would be partially built on a length artefact rather than a register effect. Demoting markdown headings as 'a human convention' (research L1158) on this basis is premature.
findings.falsifying_experiment: Recompute every flag count as flags / 1000 words and re-table the headline numbers. Separately, regenerate the AI fresh corpus with a hard word-count instruction enforced (the Tucker Max prompt asked for 9,700 words and the model produced ~1,500 — that's a generation failure, not a register finding). Until length is controlled, downgrade 'inverted signal' claims to 'unresolved.'
findings.recommendation: In dev/research/2026-04-29-genre-paired-corpus-findings.md add a length-normalised flag-rate table next to the raw count tables. Add a precondition to the demotion list (research L1154) that any pattern flagged for demotion must show no separation under both raw and length-normalised metrics.

findings.title: Selection bias unaddressed: human corpus is 5 hand-picked essays; AI corpus is generated from prompts derived FROM those essays
findings.evidence_quote: prompts L572-L637: each AI prompt is derived from the human source's content (book titles, author names, 8 specific patterns, autobiographical claims like 'realising on a Sunday afternoon you have a life philosophy'). research L1164 caveats sample size and rewriter-model identity but does NOT caveat selection of the 5 human essays.
findings.the_assumption: These 5 human essays are representative of 'long-form first-person essay' as a genre, such that what AI produces against matched prompts can stand in for 'how AI writes in this register.'
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The 5 human authors (Roberts, Sylvester-Bradley, Forte, Critchlow, Max) are all practitioner-bloggers / Medium writers / niche-journal contributors who already have an idiosyncratic blogosphere style. They are not, e.g., New Yorker essayists or peer-reviewed humanities authors. Their style might already be closer to the LLM training distribution (which includes Medium and Substack heavily) than a more diverse human sample would be. If you pick a corpus that's already adjacent to AI's mode, then conclude 'humans and AI write similarly,' you're partly measuring the corpus selection. There is no documented inclusion criterion beyond 'long-form first-person essay.'
findings.consequence_if_wrong: Reframing the skill's central voice ('audit voice from AI tell to review priority' — research L1093, README L107-L110) is grounded in 'humans use these patterns too.' If the humans in question are practitioner-bloggers whose register has been heavily ingested by LLMs, the reframe generalises to a register that doesn't represent the skill's actual users (who include UX writers, government writers, technical writers, fiction).
findings.falsifying_experiment: Add 5 human essays from explicitly non-LLM-adjacent sources: a peer-reviewed humanities journal, a print-magazine essayist (NYRB, LRB, n+1), a Government style guide author. Re-run the comparison. If strong-flag rates on this second human set are notably lower than on the practitioner-blogger set, the 'humans and AI fire equally' finding is corpus-bound, not register-bound.
findings.recommendation: Document the human-corpus inclusion procedure explicitly in dev/research/2026-04-29-genre-paired-corpus-findings.md (currently absent). Add a caveat that the 5 human essays are practitioner-blogger / Medium-adjacent and not a stratified sample. Treat 'audit voice reframe' as conditional on at least one cross-register replication.

findings.title: New candidate signals (ghost-spectral, negation density, unicode flair) are hypotheses with N=2 evidence, not validated discriminators
findings.evidence_quote: research L1132-L1142 table: ghost-spectral fires 0/5 hum, 2/5 fresh, 0/5 rewrite; negation-density 0/5, 2/5, 0/5; unicode-flair 0/5, 2/5, 0/5. README L242 'Adding new candidate signals (sentence-length mean, ghost-spectral density, negation density, unicode flair) where evidence supports.'
findings.the_assumption: Patterns that fired only on AI-fresh in this corpus are genuine AI discriminators worth promoting.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The pattern fired on 2 of 5 AI-fresh essays and 0 elsewhere. With 5 samples, a 2/5 vs 0/5 split is one essay away from 1/5 vs 1/5 — i.e., not significant by any reasonable test. Worse, the pattern fires zero times on AI-rewrite, which is the 'load-bearing' AI group per the rest of the document. So a signal that catches some AI-fresh but no AI-rewrite is identifying a sub-mode of one model's style on one prompt run, not a robust AI tell. Promoting it bakes corpus noise into the grader.
findings.consequence_if_wrong: Adding three weakly-evidenced signals while demoting four more strongly-evidenced ones is a net signal loss. The implementation plan (dev/plans/2026-04-28-human-eyes-implementation.md) doesn't gate the additions on validation, so they could land in the next iteration as 'evidence supports' when the evidence is 2 essays.
findings.falsifying_experiment: Before promoting any new check, hold it back from the iteration corpus and run it on a separate held-out N>=10 set (the 18 ai-XX samples are noted as held-out in the implementation plan). If ghost-spectral / negation / unicode flair don't fire on at least 30% of the held-out AI samples and <5% of human samples, drop them.
findings.recommendation: In README L240-L243 (the 'What's next' bullet), explicitly mark the new candidate signals as 'hypothesis-stage, requires validation on held-out corpus.' Add a corresponding gate to dev/plans/2026-04-28-human-eyes-implementation.md step 5 (Iterate based on iteration-2 results).

findings.title: The reframe is rationalisation, not progress, until a behaviour change is specified
findings.evidence_quote: research L1097 'audit voice from AI tell, fix it to review priority — verify this is working in your context.'  research L1101 'It does not loosen the rewrite policy.'  README L107-L110 'Two audiences, two rules ... To itself, when rewriting: still strip them by default.'
findings.the_assumption: Reframing audit text from 'AI tell, fix it' to 'review priority — verify this is working' is meaningful change.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The two-audiences split is structurally a no-op: the audit voice softens, the rewrite policy stays identical, and the same patterns still fire under the same severities. A user reading 'review priority' will still go through the same audit-then-rewrite flow; an AI agent rewriting still strips the same patterns. So what actually changes? If the answer is 'the user feels less scolded,' that's a copy edit, not a finding-driven product change. Worse, the reframe risks making the audit *less* useful: 'review priority' is vaguer than 'AI tell,' and the writer now has to apply judgement the previous voice handled for them. There is no test or metric that would tell the team whether the reframe improved anything.
findings.consequence_if_wrong: The team executes a multi-file rewrite (SKILL.md voice, grade.py templates, patterns.md framing) for a change that produces no measurable behaviour delta. Effort goes into framing while the underlying signal-validation work (length-normalisation, larger N, held-out sets) remains undone.
findings.falsifying_experiment: Define one observable that should change after the reframe lands. E.g., 'on the human-guardrail evals, the fraction of audit outputs that the user marks as helpful rises from X% to Y%.' If no such observable can be defined, the reframe is voice-styling, not a feature change, and shouldn't be presented as the headline takeaway from the research.
findings.recommendation: In dev/research/2026-04-29-readme-research-section-intent.md (the section-intent doc), require a measurable success criterion for the reframe before it ships to README. In dev/plans/2026-04-28-human-eyes-implementation.md, the reframe should appear as a step with a pass/fail test, not as a 'follow-up' bullet.

findings.title: Two plans from the same date: stale plan still in tree, supersedes header is structural but the in-tree plan contradicts current SKILL.md
findings.evidence_quote: implementation L654 'supersedes: dev/plans/2026-04-28-human-eyes-refocus.md'.  refocus L873-L880 still describes a 3-mode dial (Light / Balanced / All) and 'Light' as a real intensity name.  implementation L686 'Depth-setting names are ~~Obvious / Balanced / All~~ Balanced / All.'  refocus L1030 outstanding decisions still listing 'Intensity names. ... pick.'  refocus L1014-L1022 has open placeholders ('[what it does]') in the identity statement.
findings.the_assumption: Frontmatter 'supersedes' is enough to mark refocus.md as historical, and readers will know to ignore its contents.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The superseded plan is 245 lines of detailed structure with no 'SUPERSEDED — DO NOT EXECUTE' banner at the top of the body. A future maintainer (human or agent) doing repo-grep for 'depth dial' or 'severity table' finds two contradicting answers and can't tell which is current without reading frontmatter that's easy to miss. Worse, the superseded plan contains decisions the implementation plan reversed (3-point dial collapsed to 2; Audit asks the user to choose; em-dash special case folded). Leaving them documented as 'the plan' invites someone to re-implement the wrong version. The brainstorm-then-implementation plan path is fine; the issue is the superseded plan contains live-looking instructions.
findings.consequence_if_wrong: Future iteration (or an agent invoked with this repo as context) reads refocus.md, picks up the 3-point dial / 'Light' intensity, and re-implements decisions Mae explicitly reversed. This kind of ping-pong is in Mae's known-failure-mode list.
findings.falsifying_experiment: Hand the repo + a prompt 'execute the human-eyes refocus plan' to a fresh agent with no other context. If the agent picks refocus.md or merges decisions from both, the supersession is not actually working.
findings.recommendation: Add a top-of-body banner to dev/plans/2026-04-28-human-eyes-refocus.md: '> SUPERSEDED 2026-04-29 by dev/plans/2026-04-28-human-eyes-implementation.md. Do not execute. Retained for journey context only.' The frontmatter alone is not load-bearing enough.

findings.title: Counter-hypothesis the document doesn't address: AI rewrite triggers more flags BECAUSE the rewriter introduces tells, not because the original signals are broken
findings.evidence_quote: research L1083 (table showing AI rewrite mean strong = 2.6 vs human 1.8).  README L98-L101 'Asking AI to rewrite a human essay produces output with more strong-tell patterns than the human original — about 44% more in our matched comparison.'  research caveat L1166 'AI rewrite set was generated by an unspecified model; results may shift with a different rewriter.'
findings.the_assumption: The fact that strong signals fire more on AI-rewrites of human essays than on the originals shows the signals are 'register-coded not authorship-coded' (because the rewrites' authorship is partly the human's).
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: An equally plausible reading: the rewriter is a known-bad LLM that adds tells, and we're measuring those added tells correctly. In that reading the strong signals are working as designed; they detect AI fingerprints, and an AI rewrite that imports AI fingerprints into a human draft is a true positive. The document does not present this counter-hypothesis or rule it out. The leap from 'AI rewrites trigger more flags' to 'patterns are register-coded' silently requires that the AI rewrite preserves the human's register. If the rewriter just imposes its own register on the substance, then the comparison measures the rewriter's tells, not the patterns' validity. The research doc's own caveat ('results may shift with a different rewriter') concedes this without following it through.
findings.consequence_if_wrong: If the strong signals are correctly catching AI tells in AI rewrites, demoting them based on the same data destroys real signal. The reframe inverts: the data supports the existing pattern set, not its retirement.
findings.falsifying_experiment: Run the same AI rewriter on the AI-fresh outputs (so the input is already AI). If strong-flag counts go up again under rewrite, the rewriter is adding tells regardless of source register, which means the original signals are detecting the rewriter's contribution accurately. Conversely, run the same human originals through 2-3 different rewriter models. If +44% holds across rewriters, it's about the act of AI rewriting; if it varies wildly, it's about the specific rewriter.
findings.recommendation: In dev/research/2026-04-29-genre-paired-corpus-findings.md, add a 'Counter-hypothesis' section that states this alternative explicitly and notes which falsifying experiment would distinguish them. The current 'Caveats' section (L1163-L1168) names the issue but doesn't engage with it as a competing explanation.

findings.title: Implementation plan asserts 'Iteration 2 ... 86.6% mean pass rate' as success but the success bar (>=80% per case) was set after the data
findings.evidence_quote: implementation L679 'Current skill scored 86.6% mean pass rate'.  L767 'Iteration loop converges with each case at >=80% assertion pass rate (or Mae's qualitative sign-off, whichever is the gating criterion).'  L766 'The new SKILL.md fits comfortably under ~250 lines (currently 314 — see iteration 1 for trimming).'
findings.the_assumption: The 86.6% mean and the success criteria together establish the iteration is converging.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: (a) The success criterion is per-case >=80%, not mean >=80%. Reporting the mean and calling it converged hides whether the worst case is below the bar. (b) The 250-line SKILL.md target is missed by ~25% (314 vs 250) and the plan files this as 'see iteration 1 for trimming' rather than as a failure to meet the success criterion. The plan effectively self-grades by adjusting interpretation of the bar after seeing the result. (c) The 'qualitative sign-off, whichever is the gating criterion' clause makes the bar unfalsifiable: if 80% isn't met, qualitative sign-off applies; if line count is missed, that becomes a future task. There is no condition under which this plan can be said to have failed.
findings.consequence_if_wrong: An iteration loop without a stable, pre-committed pass bar can run indefinitely with 'progress' framing for arbitrarily long. Decisions to ship are then about fatigue rather than evidence.
findings.falsifying_experiment: List the per-case pass rates from iteration 2 and check whether each one >= 80%. If any case is below, the plan should report 'not yet converged' rather than '86.6% mean.' Independently, treat the 250-line target as a hard bar: if it's missed, mark the success criterion as not yet met and link to the trimming task rather than narrating around it.
findings.recommendation: In dev/plans/2026-04-28-human-eyes-implementation.md update the iteration-2 status (L679) to report (a) per-case pass rates with a column showing whether each meets the >=80% bar, (b) which success criteria from L766-L772 are met / unmet. The narrative '86.6% mean' is misleading vs the stated bar.

findings.title: Hidden assumption: the audit + suggestions + rewrite + write split assumes a turn-taking conversational shell that headless invocation will not provide
findings.evidence_quote: brainstorm L429 'After presenting the audit, the agent asks the writer whether to proceed with Suggestions, Rewrite, or stop.'  L466 'The agent asks the writer for a depth setting when invoking Rewrite or Write, unless the writer specified one.'  brainstorm L538 outstanding question (deferred): 'Headless / programmatic invocation contract. What's the default action when there's no human turn-taking?' Implementation plan does not list this question as resolved.
findings.the_assumption: There is always a writer available to answer 'Suggestions / Rewrite / stop' and 'which depth.' The brainstorm explicitly defers what happens when there isn't.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: Skills get invoked from agent loops, CI pipelines, batch jobs, and programmatic harnesses (the project's own iteration harness is one). In any non-interactive context, an agent that 'asks the writer' will either hang, hallucinate the user's choice, or default silently. The current contract gives no explicit answer for any of these. If the default is 'audit only' (as the superseded plan suggests at L1034), then any programmatic 'rewrite this' invocation degrades to audit, which is a silent failure. If the default is 'rewrite at Balanced,' then any audit-only intent is silently mutated. Either way the spec has a hole; the brainstorm names it; the implementation plan files it as 'still open' without a placeholder default.
findings.consequence_if_wrong: First headless caller hits the gap and either gets unexpected behaviour (pipeline regression) or has to read the SKILL.md trying to find a non-existent answer. The eval harness itself depends on programmatic invocation; if the contract is unclear, eval results may be measuring the test harness's defaults rather than the skill's intended behaviour.
findings.falsifying_experiment: Try to invoke the skill on a file via the iteration harness with no conversational context and an instruction 'rewrite at All.' Observe whether the skill rewrites, audits, or stalls. Whichever happens, ask whether SKILL.md describes that behaviour. If not, headless invocation is undefined.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit headless-invocation contract to dev/plans/2026-04-28-human-eyes-implementation.md (or move the brainstorm's deferred question to resolved). State which action runs when no user turn-taking is possible, what the default depth is, and how the skill signals that a needed choice was assumed.

findings.title: README presents '+44%' as load-bearing claim while underlying research reports it as 'directional, not definitive' — internal contradiction in epistemic claims
findings.evidence_quote: README L121-L127 'Mean pass rate: 95.5% across 18 evals\n- Human-vs-ai_rewrite flag gap: total -12% / strong +44%\n... The human-vs-ai gap lines are the load-bearing claim'.  README L233 'Sample sizes are small. Findings here are based on a 15-sample matched corpus ... Directional, not definitive.'  research L1163 'n=5 per group. Findings are directional; need larger N before being baked into the grader.'
findings.the_assumption: It's coherent to call the same number both 'load-bearing' and 'directional, not definitive.'
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: 'Load-bearing' means the rest of the structure rests on it. 'Directional' means it indicates a possible direction but doesn't establish anything. A claim cannot do both jobs; if the gap is load-bearing it must be definitive enough to bear weight, and if it's directional it cannot bear weight without snapping. The README places +44% as the central performance metric while the same README's 'Where to be careful' section acknowledges it's directional. A reader who reads top-to-bottom is given a confident headline number, then told not to take it seriously. This isn't a calibration nuance; it's two contradictory epistemic stances on the same datum, in the same document.
findings.consequence_if_wrong: Readers who trust the headline take +44% as established and update the skill's reputation accordingly. Readers who reach the caveat realise the headline was overstated. Internally, the team uses '+44%' to justify pattern demotions while elsewhere admitting the data isn't strong enough to act on. The two readings cannot both be correct, and any decision the team makes citing one will be undermined by the other.
findings.falsifying_experiment: Decide which stance is right. Either: (a) the data is strong enough to be load-bearing, in which case the 'directional, not definitive' caveat must be removed and per-case CIs supplied; or (b) the data is directional, in which case +44% must be moved out of headline position and reframed as 'preliminary.' Holding both is not a viable position for a public README.
findings.recommendation: Pick one. If the team genuinely believes +44% is load-bearing, supply CIs and per-case data and remove the directional caveat. If the team believes the finding is directional, move the +44% number out of the Performance block and replace with a 'preliminary indication' framing. The current state is a credibility liability.

residual_risks: The corpus's 'matched-pair' design is anchored on 5 human authors whose register may already overlap with LLM training distributions; replication on a non-blogger human set is required before any of the reframe-driven changes generalise.
The implementation plan's iteration loop has no published termination condition that's robust to the 'qualitative sign-off' escape hatch (ADV-009). The plan can run indefinitely.

Pattern demotions and new candidate signals would ship together as 'evidence-driven' when both rest on the same N=5 corpus that the document concedes is too small. Net effect on grader signal is unmeasured.
testing_gaps: No length-normalised flag-rate computation is reported. The whole research finding is sensitive to this and the data is in hand.
No bootstrap or even basic CI on the means in the headline table. With N=5, this is not optional, it's the minimum.
No held-out validation of the new candidate signals (ghost-spectral, negation density, unicode flair) before the implementation plan promotes them.
No counter-hypothesis check that the +44% rewrite gap is rewriter-specific (only one model used; impact of changing rewriter not tested).
No measurement of whether the audit-voice reframe changes user behaviour (no observable defined; no before/after metric in the plan).
Headless / programmatic invocation contract is unspecified, which means the existing eval harness and any third-party programmatic caller may be exercising undocumented defaults.
